design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate overworld, recruit units, engage in turn-based tactical battles across two parallel campaigns (Alm and Celica's armies) to resolve conflict between divine powers
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Ideological divide between two protagonists: Alm pursues military victory while Celica seeks spiritual/peaceful resolution; both armies converge toward truth about gods Duma and Mila's corruption
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command two distinct armies across a continent, experience dual perspectives on the same conflict, unlock class flexibility and customization through leveling
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately diverges from predecessor's formula by adding navigable overworld, role-playing elements (town interaction, trading), and permitting flexible class promotion mid-campaign rather than fixed progressions. Director Shouzou Kaga explicitly designed this to address pacing/character connection issues from Shadow Dragon, making it controversial as the series' 'black sheep'

difficulty.lives_system: Permadeath for individual units (inherited Fire Emblem mechanic); no explicit all-unit game over documented
difficulty.checkpointing: Chapter-based progression with overworld navigation between battles; no mid-battle saves documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Two explicit difficulty modes available: 'Normal' and 'Easy'. Easy mode doubles experience points and permits item exchange between units beyond vendor constraints, providing direct mechanical accommodation for accessibility

progression.upgrade_system: Character stat growth through leveling with percentile-based growth rates unique per character; spell acquisition via leveling (magic classes learn spells automatically); health-to-mana conversion system (spells consume caster health)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Class promotion unlocked upon reaching specific experience levels; Alm promotes Fighter→Hero, Celica promotes Priestess→Princess; flexible mid-campaign class switching enabled for other units. Overworld areas progressively open as story advances
replayability_hooks: Dual protagonist campaigns offer narrative branching perspective
Permadeath mechanic creates variable roster composition across playthroughs
Flexible class system allows different character build configurations
Easy/Normal difficulty modes provide mechanical variation

world_building_techniques: Dual campaign structure (Alm vs Celica) creating unreliable narrator effect—player experiences conflicting perspectives on same events
Divine/mythological framework: two gods (Mila/Duma) with opposing philosophies corrupting their respective kingdoms (Zofia/Rigel)
Town NPCs and dialogue providing exposition on coup d'état (Desaix), religious schism, resource drought caused by Mila's disappearance
Character relationship discovery (Alm/Celica childhood friendship revelation, Alm's hidden parentage as Rudolf's son)
Environmental storytelling through overworld navigation connecting battles to inhabited spaces

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters tied to narrative/political structure: Desaix (usurper), Rudolf (invader emperor with hidden noble intent), Duma (mad god). Bosses represent ideological obstacles rather than purely mechanical challenges
notable_bosses: Desaix - triggers Zofia's internal conflict in Chapter 1
Grieth - carves desert stronghold, fought during Celica's Chapter 3
Dolth - 'Great Cantor' serving dark sorcery faction, Celica's Chapter 4
Emperor Rudolf - ultimate antagonist revealed as Alm's biological father; death triggers revelation about gods' insanity
Duma - final boss, the War Father god; defeated jointly by Alm and Celica

faction_indicators: Kingdom of Zofia (Mila-worshipping, matriarchal: Princess Celica leads pilgrimage)
Empire of Rigel (Duma-worshipping, martial: Emperor Rudolf commands invasion)
Chancellor Desaix's usurper faction (internal Zofian coup)
danger_signals: Territorial invasion by Rigel across Valentia
Internal coup attempt by military leadership (Desaix) exploiting power vacuum
Divine madness: both Mila and Duma have fallen into insanity through unchecked power
Resource collapse: Mila's disappearance causes widespread drought in Zofia
Ideological schism between pragmatism (Alm's military solution) and spiritual seeking (Celica's pilgrimage)
environmental_storytelling: Overworld towns accessible for recruitment, trade, NPC dialogue revealing backstory
Desert shrine locations (Grieth's Fort) representing alternative power centers
Duma Temple as final dungeon, mythic site of divine corruption
Open sea regions (Chapter 2) featuring pirate encounters, reflecting resource scarcity
Zofia Castle as meeting point where protagonists part ways ideologically

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Fire Emblem Gaiden (unused content, development notes, difficulty select documentation)
Wikipedia - Fire Emblem Gaiden (gameplay systems, synopsis, development context, director intent)
